elements.text: Credit reports may include accounts identified as possible non-applicant accounts (or with other similar notation). Non-applicant accounts may belong to the borrower, or they may truly belong to another individual.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Typical causes of non-applicant accounts include:
elements.label: text

elements.text: applicants who are Juniors or Seniors,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: individuals who move frequently,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: unrelated individuals who have identical names, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: debts the borrower applied for under a different Social Security number or under a different address. These may be indicative of potential fraud.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the debts do not belong to the borrower, the lender may provide supporting documentation to validate this, and may exclude the non-applicant debts for the borrower's DTI ratio. If the debts do belong to the borrower, they must be included as part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Deferred Installment Debt
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Deferred installment debts must be included as part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations. For deferred installment debts other than student loans, if the borrower's credit report does not indicate the monthly amount that will be payable at the end of the deferment period, the lender must obtain copies of the borrower's payment letters or forbearance agreements so that a monthly payment amount can be determined and used in calculating the borrower's total monthly obligations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For information about deferred student loans, see Student Loans below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Federal Income Tax Installment Agreements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a borrower has entered into or applied for an installment agreement with the IRS to repay delinquent federal income taxes, and there is no indication that a Notice of Federal Tax Lien has been filed against the borrower in the county in which the subject property is located, the lender must obtain the following documentation:
elements.label: text

elements.text: For installment agreements approved by the IRS
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a copy of the approved IRS installment agreement with the terms of repayment, including the monthly payment amount and total amount due; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: evidence the borrower is current on the payments associated with the tax installment plan. Acceptable evidence includes the most recent payment reminder from the IRS, reflecting the last payment amount and date and the next payment amount owed and due date.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For installment agreements pending approval by the IRS
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a copy of the application for the installment agreement with the terms of repayment, including the monthly payment amount and total amount due.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: In both cases, if the borrower is not paying off the amount owed in full, the lender must include the monthly payment amount as part of the borrower's monthly debt obligations. If any of the above conditions are not met, the borrower must pay off the outstanding balance due under the installment agreement with the IRS in accordance with B3-6-07, Debts Paid Off At or Prior to Closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The payments on a federal income tax installment agreement approved by the IRS can be excluded from the borrower's DTI ratio if the agreement meets the terms in Debts Paid by Others or Installment Debt in this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: As a reminder, the lender remains responsible under the life-of-loan representations and warranties for clear title and first-lien enforceability in accordance with A2-2-07, Life-of-Loan Representations and Warranties.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Garnishments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: All garnishments with more than ten months remaining must be included in the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations for qualifying purposes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Home Equity Lines of Credit
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When the mortgage that will be delivered to Fannie Mae also has a home equity line of credit (HELOC) that provides for a monthly payment of principal and interest or interest only, the payment on the HELOC must be considered as part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations. If the HELOC does not require a payment, there is no recurring monthly debt obligation so the lender does not need to develop an equivalent payment amount.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Installment Debt
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: All installment debt that is not secured by a financial asset-including student loans, automobile loans, personal loans, and timeshares-must be considered part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations if there are more than ten monthly payments remaining. However, an installment debt with fewer monthly payments remaining also should be considered as a recurring monthly debt obligation if it significantly affects the borrower's ability to meet their credit obligations. See below for treatment of payments due under a federal income tax installment agreement.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: A timeshare account should be treated as an installment debt regardless of how it is reported on the credit report or other documentation (that is, even if reported as a mortgage loan).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lease Payments
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lease payments must be considered as recurring monthly debt obligations regardless of the number of months remaining on the lease. This is because the expiration of a lease agreement for rental housing or an automobile typically leads to either a new lease agreement, the buyout of the existing lease, or the purchase of a new vehicle or house.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Rental Housing Payment
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The housing payment for each borrower's principal residence must be considered when underwriting the loan. For the following scenarios, the borrower's monthly rental housing payment must be evaluated (if the borrower does not otherwise have a mortgage payment or no housing expense):
elements.label: text

elements.text: for non-occupant borrowers, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: for second homes or investment properties.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The following list provides examples of acceptable documentation to verify the rental payment:
elements.label: text

elements.text: six months canceled checks or equivalent payment source;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: six months bank statements reflecting a clear and consistent payment to an organization or individual;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: direct verification of rent from a management company or individual landlord; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a copy of a current, fully executed lease agreement and two months canceled checks (or equivalent payment source) supporting the rental payment amount.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note: Refer to B3-5.4-03, Documentation and Assessment of a Nontraditional Credit History for rental payment history requirements when using non-traditional credit.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Loans Secured by Financial Assets
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a borrower uses their financial assets-life insurance policies, 401(k) accounts, individual retirement accounts, certificates of deposit, stocks, bonds, etc.-as security for a loan, the borrower has a contingent liability.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender is not required to include this contingent liability as part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations provided the lender obtains a copy of the applicable loan instrument that shows the borrower's financial asset as collateral for the loan. If the borrower intends to use the same asset to satisfy financial reserve requirements, the lender must reduce the value of the asset (the account balance, in most cases) by the proceeds from the secured loan and any related fees to determine whether the borrower has sufficient reserves.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: Payment on any debt secured by virtual currency is an exception to the above policy and must be included when calculating the debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Open 30-Day Charge Accounts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Open 30-day charge accounts require the balance to be paid in full every month. Fannie Mae does not require open 30-day charge accounts to be included in the debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: See B3-6-07, Debts Paid Off At or Prior to Closing, for additional information on open 30-day charge accounts.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Other Real Estate Owned-Qualifying Impact
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For details regarding the qualifying impact of other real estate owned, see B3-6-06, Qualifying Impact of Other Real Estate Owned.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Revolving Charge/Lines of Credit
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Revolving charge accounts and unsecured lines of credit are open-ended and must be treated as long-term debts and considered part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations. These tradelines include credit cards and department store charge cards, including accounts on which the borrower is an authorized user, and
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: personal lines of credit. Equity lines of credit secured by real estate should be included in the housing expense.
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the credit report does not show a required minimum payment amount and there is no supplemental documentation to support a payment of less than 5%, the lender must use 5% of the outstanding balance as the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligation.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For DU loan casefiles, if a revolving debt is provided on the loan application without a monthly payment amount, DU will use the greater of $10 or 5% of the outstanding balance as the monthly payment when calculating the total debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Student Loans
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If a monthly student loan payment is provided on the credit report, the lender may use that amount for qualifying purposes. If the credit report does not reflect the correct monthly payment, the lender may use the monthly payment that is on the student loan documentation (the most recent student loan statement) to qualify the borrower.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the credit report does not provide a monthly payment for the student loan, or if the credit report shows $0 as the monthly payment, the lender must determine the qualifying monthly payment using one of the options below.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the borrower is on an income-driven payment plan, the lender may obtain student loan documentation to verify the actual monthly payment is $0. The lender may then qualify the borrower with a $0 payment.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: For deferred loans or loans in forbearance, the lender may calculate
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a payment equal to 1% of the outstanding student loan balance (even if this amount is lower than the actual fully amortizing payment), or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a fully amortizing payment using the documented loan repayment terms.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: B3-6-06, Qualifying Impact of Other Real Estate Owned (06/30/2015)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic describes the qualifying impact of other real estate owned, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Qualifying Considerations
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Mortgage Assumption
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Property Settlement Buyout
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Current Principal Residence Pending Sale
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Qualifying Considerations
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When the borrower owns mortgaged real estate, the status of the property determines how the existing property's PITIA must be considered in qualifying for the new mortgage transaction. If the mortgaged property owned by the borrower is
elements.label: text

elements.text: an existing investment property or a current principal residence converting to investment use, the borrower must be qualified in accordance with, but not limited to, the policies in topics B3-3.8-01, Rental Income, B3-4.1-01, Minimum Reserve Requirements, and, if applicable B2-2-03, Multiple Financed Properties for the Same Borrower;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: an existing second home or a current principal residence converting to a second home, the PITIA of the second home must also be counted as part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the borrower's current principal residence that is pending sale but will not close (with title transfer to the new owner) prior to the subject transaction, the lender must comply with the policies in this topic.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: In conjunction with the policies in this topic, the lender must also comply with the policies in B2-2-03, Multiple Financed Properties for the Same Borrower, B3-3.8-01, Rental Income, and B3-4.1-01, Minimum Reserve Requirements, as applicable.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Mortgage Assumption
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a borrower sells a mortgaged property and the property purchaser assumes the outstanding mortgage debt without a release of liability, the borrower has a contingent liability.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender is not required to count this contingent liability (PITIA) as part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations if the lender verifies that the property purchaser has at least a 12-month history of making regular, timely payments for the mortgage. The lender can document this by obtaining
elements.label: text

elements.text: evidence of the transfer of ownership;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a copy of the formal, executed assumption agreement; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a credit report indicating that consistent and timely payments were made for the assumed mortgage.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the lender cannot document timely payments during the most recent 12-month period, the applicable mortgage payment must be counted as part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Property Settlement Buyout
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a borrower's interest in a property is bought out by another co-owner of the property, as often happens in a divorce settlement, but the lender does not release the borrower from liability under the mortgage, the borrower has a contingent liability.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the lender obtains documentation to confirm the transfer of title to the property, this liability does not have to be considered as part of the borrower's recurring monthly debt obligations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Current Principal Residence Pending Sale
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the borrower's current principal residence is pending sale, but the transaction will not close with title transfer to the new owner prior to the subject transaction, and the borrower is purchasing a new principal residence, the current PITIA and the proposed PITIA must be used in qualifying the borrower for the new mortgage loan.
elements.label: text

elements.text: However, Fannie Mae will not require the current principal residence's PITIA to be used in qualifying the borrower as long as the following documentation is provided:
elements.label: text

elements.text: the executed sales contract for the current residence, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: confirmation that any financing contingencies have been cleared.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Recent Related Announcements
elements.label: text

elements.text: There are no recently issued Announcements related to this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: B3-6-07, Debts Paid Off At or Prior to Closing (04/05/2023)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on debts paid off at or prior to closing, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Payoff or Paydown of Debt for Qualification
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Open 30-Day Charge Accounts
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Collections, Charge-Offs of Non-Mortgage Accounts, Judgments, and Liens
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Payoff or Paydown of Debt for Qualification
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Payoff or paydown of debt solely to qualify must be carefully evaluated and considered in the overall loan analysis. The borrower's history of credit use should be a factor in determining whether the appropriate approach is to include or exclude debt for qualification. Generally
elements.label: text

elements.text: Installment loans that are being paid off or paid down to 10 or fewer remaining monthly payments do not need to be included in the borrower's long-term debt.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If a revolving account balance is to be paid off at or prior to closing, a monthly payment on the current outstanding balance does not need to be included in the borrower's long-term debt, i.e., not included in the debt-to-income (DTI) ratio. Such accounts do not need to be closed as a condition of excluding the payment from the DTI ratio.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: See B3-6-02, Debt-to-Income Ratios for additional guidance on calculating total monthly obligations for qualifying purposes.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Open 30-Day Charge Accounts
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For open 30-day charge accounts that do not reflect a monthly payment on the credit report, or 30-day accounts that reflect a monthly payment that is identical to the account balance, lenders must verify borrower funds to cover the account balance. The verified funds must be in addition to any funds required for closing costs and reserves.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : DU will include the balance of the 30-day charge accounts on the loan application in the Reserves Required to be Verified amount shown on the DU Underwriting Findings report. However, for transactions that do not require the verification of reserves, the balance of 30-day charge accounts in the Reserves Required to be Verified amount will be reduced by any cash out the borrower will receive through the transaction.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the borrower paid off the account balance prior to closing, the lender may provide proof of payoff in lieu of verifying funds to cover the account balance.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Collections, Charge-Offs of Non-Mortgage Accounts, Judgments, and Liens
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Delinquent credit-including taxes, judgments, charge-offs of non-mortgage accounts (see below for exceptions), tax liens, mechanic's or materialmen's liens, and liens that have the potential to affect Fannie Mae's lien position or diminish the borrower's equity-must be paid off at or prior to closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Delinquent federal income taxes that are approved to be paid by a monthly installment agreement with the IRS must be paid in full at or prior to closing if there is any indication that a Notice of Federal Tax Lien has been recorded against the borrower in the county in which the subject property is located. For additional information about federal income tax installment agreements, see  B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For details regarding delinquent federal income taxes that the IRS has approved to be paid through an installment agreement that can be included as a monthly debt obligation, rather than being paid in full, also see B3-6-05, Monthly Debt Obligations.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For manually underwritten loans, non-medical collection accounts and charge-offs on non-mortgage accounts do not have to be paid off at or prior to closing if the balance of an individual account is less than $250 or the total balance of all accounts is $1,000 or less. Non-medical collection accounts and charge-offs on non-mortgage accounts that exceed these limits must be paid off at or prior to closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: For DU underwritten loans, refer to  B3-5.3-09, DU Credit Report Analysis.
elements.label: text

elements.text: B3-6-08, DU: Requirements for Liability Assessment (01/27/2015)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on DU requirements for liability assessment, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Reconciling the Loan Application with the Credit Report
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Auto-Populating DU Liabilities from the Credit Report
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Reconciling the Loan Application with the Credit Report
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: DU uses liabilities from the loan application, not debts from the credit report, to calculate the debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: To help ensure that all appropriate liabilities are included in the debt-to-income ratio, DU performs a series of reasonableness tests comparing loan application balances and payments with the credit report balances and payments. If the values on the loan application are less than the values on the credit report by more than selected tolerances, the lender must justify the discrepancies between the two. The lender must update the loan application values if the values are needed to calculate accurate ratios. The information must be updated either with verified values from the credit report or with independent, outside verifications.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Auto-Populating DU Liabilities from the Credit Report
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender can automatically copy the borrower's liabilities from the credit report to the loan application by selecting the auto-populate liabilities option from DU when the credit report is ordered. If the lender's loan origination system does not offer this option, or if the lender elects not to use it, the liabilities must be entered manually into the loan application.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When the auto-populate option is selected, it is not necessary to obtain additional borrower disclosure for tradelines appearing on the credit report. The lender is still required to obtain full disclosure from all borrowers, including borrowers who do not have traditional credit, of all existing credit obligations. Liabilities that do not appear on the credit report, such as monthly housing expenses for taxes, insurance, must be disclosed in the loan application prior to final submission to DU.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If the auto-populate liabilities option is selected BEFORE liabilities have been manually entered in the loan application:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Open accounts will be automatically copied to the loan application.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Closed accounts on the credit report are not automatically copied to the loan application. If the account has an outstanding balance, the lender must manually enter the liability in the loan application and include the monthly payment in the debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Collection accounts on the credit report are not automatically copied to the loan application.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the auto-populate liabilities option is selected AFTER liabilities have been manually entered in the loan application:
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU will attempt to match existing liability accounts listed on the loan application to the credit report liabilities by using a combination of account name and account number.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Open accounts from the credit report that were not manually entered on the loan application will be automatically copied to the loan application.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU will use the information on the loan application to calculate the debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If duplicate accounts or accounts that do not belong to the borrower were copied to the loan application and included in the debt-to-income ratio, they may be omitted (or deleted) from the loan application. Debts that are omitted will not be counted in the debt-to-income ratio.
elements.label: text

elements.label: text

elements.text: Subpart B4, Underwriting Property
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: This subpart contains property underwriting and appraisal requirements for conventional loans.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Chapter B4-1, Property Assessment and Valuation
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: text

elements.text: This chapter explains Fannie Mae's property assessment and valuation requirements, including documentation, appraisal and data collection requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Section B4-1.1, General Appraisal Requirements
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: B4-1.1-01, Definition of Market Value (06/04/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the definition of market value.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Definition of Market Value
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Definition of Market Value
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Market value is the most probable price that a property should bring in a competitive and open market under all conditions requisite to a fair sale with, the buyer and seller, each acting prudently and knowledgeably, and assuming the price is not affected by undue stimulus. Implicit in this definition is the consummation of a sale as of a specified date and the passing of title from seller to buyer under conditions whereby:
elements.label: text

elements.text: buyer and seller are typically motivated;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: both parties are well informed or well advised, and each acting in what they consider to be in their own best interest;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a reasonable time is allowed for exposure in the open market;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: payment is made in terms of cash in U.S. dollars or in terms of financial arrangements comparable thereto; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: the price represents the normal consideration for the property sold unaffected by special or creative financing or sales concessions granted by anyone associated with the sale.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note : Adjustments to the comparables must be made for special or creative financing or sales concessions. No adjustments are necessary for costs that are normally paid by sellers as a result of tradition or law in a market area; these costs are readily identifiable because the seller pays these costs in virtually all sales transactions. Special or creative financing adjustments can be made to the comparable property by comparisons to financing terms offered by a third-party institutional lender that is not already involved in the property or transaction. Any adjustment should not be calculated on a mechanical dollar for dollar cost of the financing or concession, but the dollar amount of any adjustment should approximate the market's reaction to the financing or concessions based on the appraiser's judgment.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders using UAD 3.6 must follow the requirements in the UAD 3.6 Policy Supplement .
elements.label: text

elements.text: B4-1.1-02, Lender Responsibilities (06/04/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on lender requirements, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lender Responsibilities
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Confirmation and Documentation of the Current Owner
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Objective and Unbiased Appraisals
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Reporting Unfavorable Conditions
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lender Responsibilities
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender is responsible for ensuring that the subject property provides adequate collateral for the mortgage. For most loans, Fannie Mae requires that the lender obtain a signed and complete appraisal report that accurately reflects the market value, condition, and marketability of the property. Some loans may be eligible for a value acceptance option, and an appraisal is not required if the lender exercises the option and complies with the related requirements. (See B4-1.4-10, Value Acceptance and B4-1.4-11, Value Acceptance + Property Data, for additional information.)
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: If an appraisal is obtained, the lender is responsible for
elements.label: text

elements.text: providing the borrower disclosures and requirements described in B4-1.3-12, Appraisal Quality Matters;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: compliance with the Appraiser Independence Requirements ;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: selection of the appraiser (see B4-1.1-03, Appraiser Selection Criteria);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: compliance with the Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) when applicable (see B4-1.1-06, Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) and the Uniform Collateral Data Portal (UCDP));
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ensuring the appraiser has utilized sound reasoning and provided evidence to support the methodology chosen to develop the value opinion, particularly in cases that are not covered by Fannie Mae policy;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: successful submission of the appraisal through the UCDP prior to sale of the loan (see B4-1.1-06, Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) and the Uniform Collateral Data Portal (UCDP));
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: continually evaluating the appraiser's work through the quality control process (see B4-1.3-12, Appraisal Quality Matters and Subpart D1, Lender QC Process ); and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ensuring the appraisal does not contain subjective and prohibited language relating to discriminatory practices and appraisal bias.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the transaction involves property data collection, the lender is responsible for
elements.label: text

elements.text: compliance with the Property Data Collector Independence Requirements ;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: selection of the property data collector (see B4-1.4-11, Value Acceptance + Property Data);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: successful submission of the data to Fannie Mae's Property Data API prior to loan delivery; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: continually evaluating the property data collector's work through the quality control process (see also Subpart D1, Lender QC Process ).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Confirmation and Documentation of the Current Owner
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Publicly available information helps to identify property flipping schemes, which typically involve various combinations of transactions and result in a sale of recently acquired property for significant profit based on a misleading or fraudulent appraisal with an inflated property value. The lender must confirm that the property seller in a purchase transaction (or the borrower in a refinance transaction) is the owner of the subject property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Lenders must confirm and document in the loan file that the property seller in a purchase money transaction or the borrower in a refinance transaction is the owner of the subject property when an appraisal is required. Examples of acceptable documentation include, but are not limited to:
elements.label: text

elements.text: a copy of a recorded deed, mortgage, or deed of trust,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a recent property tax bill or tax assessment notice,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a title report,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a title commitment or binder, or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: a property sale history report.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: This documentation is especially important for transactions involving an assignment (or sale) of a contract for sale and back-to-back, simultaneous, double transaction closings, or double escrows to support the property acquisition, financing, and closing.
elements.label: text

elements.text: When the transaction is part of an employee relocation, the relocation company may be the assignee of the seller, which should be indicated on the sales contract. Additionally, the appraiser must comment on this condition in the appraisal report.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Objective and Unbiased Appraisals
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: A lender must ensure the appraiser
elements.label: text

elements.text: described the property and the market area in factual, unbiased, and specific terms;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: considered all factors that have an effect on value; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: was objective and unbiased in the development of the opinion of market value in the appraisal report.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A number of federal, state, and local laws prohibit discrimination in the appraisal of housing. Fannie Mae expects professional appraisers to fully understand that discriminatory valuation and appraisal reporting practices are not only illegal, but also unethical. Unintentional discrimination can occur in the appraisal report as the result of what an appraiser states, or fails to state. The lender and appraiser must ensure the appraisal is not in violation of any unacceptable appraisal practices (see B4-1.1-04, Unacceptable Appraisal Practices).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Reporting Unfavorable Conditions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender must ensure that appraiser comments regarding unfavorable conditions, such as the existence of an adverse environmental or economic factor, also discuss how the condition affects the value or marketability of the property being appraised and explain how the condition was taken into consideration in the valuation process. In such cases, the appraiser's analysis must reflect and include comparable sales that are similarly affected whenever possible. The appraiser must address the impact these factors may have, if any, on the value and marketability of the subject property. (See B4-1.3-06, Property Condition and Quality of Construction of the Improvements, for further information).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders using UAD 3.6 must follow the requirements in the UAD 3.6 Policy Supplement .

elements.text: B4-1.1-03, Appraiser Selection Criteria (06/04/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains general information on appraiser selection, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Appraiser License and Certification
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Appraiser Trainees
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Knowledge and Experience
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Selection of the Appraiser
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Supervisory Appraiser
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Appraiser License and Certification
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae requires a lender (or its authorized agent) to use appraisers or supervisory appraisers that are statelicensed or state-certified (in accordance with the provisions of Title XI of the Financial Institutions Reform, Recovery and Enforcement Act of 1989 and all applicable state laws). The lender (or its authorized agent) must document that the appraisers it uses are licensed or certified as appropriate under the applicable state law. The lender must ensure that the state license or state certification is active as of the effective date of the appraisal report. The appraiser must note their license or certification number on the individual appraisal report forms, in compliance with the Uniform Appraisal Dataset Specification, Appendix D: Field-Specific Standardization Requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae's appraisal report forms identify the appraiser as the individual who
elements.label: text

elements.text: performed the analysis, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: prepared and signed the appraisal report as the appraiser.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: This does not preclude appraisers from relying on individuals who are not state-licensed or state-certified to provide significant professional assistance, such as an appraiser trainee or an employee of the appraiser doing market data research or data verification in the development of the appraisal. Under some state laws, a lender's use of an unlicensed or uncertified appraiser that is working as an employee or sub-contractor of a licensed or certified appraiser will satisfy the state's licensing and certification requirement, as long as the appraisal report is signed by a state-licensed or state-certified supervisory or review appraiser. The state-licensed or statecertified appraiser that signs the appraisal report must acknowledge in the report the extent of the professional assistance provided by others and the specific tasks performed by each individual, and must certify that each named individual is qualified to perform the tasks.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Appraiser Trainees
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Fannie Mae allows an unlicensed or uncertified appraiser, or trainee (or other similar classification) to perform a significant amount of the appraisal (or the entire appraisal if they are qualified to do so). If an unlicensed or uncertified individual provides significant professional assistance, they must sign the left side of the appraiser certification as the Appraiser if
elements.label: text

elements.text: they are working under the supervision of a state-licensed or state-certified appraiser as an employee or sub-contractor,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: the right side of the appraiser certification is signed by that supervisory appraiser, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: it is acceptable under state law.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If the jurisdiction does not provide license numbers for trainees, the term 'Trainee' should be entered in the 'Other' field in the Appraiser Certification section.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Knowledge and Experience
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders must use appraisers that
elements.label: text

elements.text: have the requisite knowledge required to perform a professional quality appraisal for the specific geographic location and particular property type; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: have the requisite knowledge about, and access to, all necessary and appropriate local data sources for the subject property's geographic area, including multiple listing service(s) and government records.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Appraisers that are not familiar with specific real estate markets may not have adequate information available to perform a reliable appraisal. Although the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP) allows an appraiser that does not have the appropriate knowledge and experience to accept an appraisal assignment by providing procedures with which the appraiser can complete the assignment, Fannie Mae does not allow the USPAP flexibility.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Selection of the Appraiser
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The lender
elements.label: text

elements.text: is responsible for the selection of appraisers and for the qualifications and quality of work provided by the appraisers that are selected;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: may not use appraisals ordered or received by borrowers or other parties with an interest in the transaction, such as the property seller or real estate agent. Fannie Mae does allow lenders to use thirdparty vendors (for example, appraisal management companies) to manage the appraiser selection process. However, it should be noted that if a lender enters into a contract with any vendor, contractor,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: or third-party service provider, the lender is accountable for the quality of the work performed as if it was performed by an employee of the lender.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender (or its authorized agent) must
elements.label: text

elements.text: establish policies and procedures to ensure that qualified individuals are being selected in accordance with Fannie Mae requirements, including the Appraiser Independence Requirements.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: ensure that an appraiser has demonstrated the ability to perform high-quality appraisals before using an appraiser's services. The quality of an appraiser's work is a key criterion that must be used in determining which appraiser the lender (or its authorized agent) uses for its assignments. The requirement for an appraiser to produce a high-quality work product must always outweigh fee or turnaround time considerations.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: develop and maintain a documented process to monitor the appraisers it uses. The process (at a minimum) must include an annual review of an appraiser's state licensing or certification status and a procedure for suspending or terminating business with individual appraisers. Additionally, the lender must have a procedure for referring appraisers to the applicable state appraiser licensing and regulatory board.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Delegating these responsibilities to a third party does not relieve the lender of its responsibilities related to the appraisal or the value, condition, and marketability of the property. See B4-1.3-12, Appraisal Quality Matters, for information related to ongoing review of appraisals.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note : Fannie Mae does not approve appraisers. Therefore, when selecting appraisers, lenders must not give any consideration to an appraiser's representation that they are approved or qualified by Fannie Mae.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Supervisory Appraiser
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: As noted in the License and Certification section in this topic, Fannie Mae allows an unlicensed or uncertified appraiser, or trainee (or other similar classification) that works as an employee or subcontractor of a licensed or certified appraiser, to perform a significant amount of the appraisal (or the entire appraisal if they are qualified to do so), as long as the appraisal report is signed by a licensed or certified supervisory or review appraiser and is acceptable under state law.
elements.label: text

elements.text: If a supervisory appraiser is used, the supervisory appraiser does not need to physically inspect the subject property or comparables, but must sign the right side of the report and certify that they
elements.label: text

elements.text: directly supervised the appraiser that prepared the appraisal report,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: have reviewed the appraisal report,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: agree with the statements and conclusions of the appraiser,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: agree to be bound by certifications as set forth in Fannie Mae's appraisal report forms, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: take full responsibility for the appraisal report.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: A supervisory appraiser may not sign the left-hand side of the appraisal report unless they have met the requirements of the appraiser as noted in the License and Certification section in this topic.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Lenders using UAD 3.6 must follow the requirements in the UAD 3.6 Policy Supplement .

elements.text: B4-1.1-04, Unacceptable Appraisal Practices (06/04/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains examples of unacceptable practices, many of which are reflected in the appraiser's certifications on the appraisal reports.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Unacceptable Appraisal Practices
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Unacceptable Appraisal Practices
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following are examples of unacceptable appraisal practices:
elements.label: text

elements.text: development of or reporting an opinion of market value (including responses to requests for an ROV) that is not supportable by market data or is misleading;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: development of a valuation conclusion based either partially or completely on the sex, race, color, religion, disability, national origin, familial status, or including a reference to any protected class of either the prospective owners or occupants of the subject property or the present owners or occupants of the properties in the vicinity of the subject property;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: reference to crime rate or related data;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: use of unsupported assumptions, interjections of personal opinion, or perceptions about factors in the valuation process and the use of subjective terminology, including, but not limited to:
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: "pride of ownership," "no pride of ownership," and "lack of pride of ownership";
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: "poor neighborhood";
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: "good neighborhood";
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: "crime" (and its variants);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: "desirable neighborhood or location"; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: "undesirable neighborhood or location";
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: development of a valuation conclusion based on factors that local, state, or federal law designate as discriminatory, and thus, prohibited;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: misrepresentation of the physical characteristics of the subject property, improvements, or comparable sales;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: failure to comment on negative factors with respect to the subject neighborhood, the subject property, or proximity of the subject property to adverse influences;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: failure to adequately analyze and report any current contract of sale, option, offering, or listing of the subject property and the prior sales of the subject property and the comparable sales;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: selection and use of inappropriate comparable sales;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: failure to use comparable sales that are the most locationally and physically similar to the subject property;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: creation of comparable sales by combining vacant land sales with the contract purchase price of a home that has been built or will be built on the land;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: failure to personally inspect the exterior of the comparable property when required by the scope of work in the appraisal report;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: use of adjustments to comparable sales that do not reflect market reaction to the differences between the subject property and the comparable sales;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: not supporting adjustments in the sales comparison approach;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: failure to make market-derived adjustments, including time adjustments, when they are clearly indicated;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: use of data, particularly comparable sales data, provided by parties that have a financial interest in the sale or in the financing of the subject property without the appraiser's verification of the information from a disinterested source;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: development of an appraisal or reporting an appraisal in a manner or direction that favors the cause of either the client or any related party, the amount of the opinion of value, the attainment of a specific result, or the occurrence of a subsequent event in order to receive compensation or employment for performing the appraisal or in anticipation of receiving future assignments; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: development of or reporting an appraisal in a manner that is inconsistent with the requirements of USPAP.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders using UAD 3.6 must follow the requirements in the UAD 3.6 Policy Supplement .

elements.text: B4-1.1-05, Disclosure of Information to Appraisers (06/04/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on lender disclosure of information to appraisers, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Sales Contract Information
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Information Disclosed to the Appraiser
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Contract Changes After the Appraisal is Completed
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: All information about the subject property the lender is aware of must be disclosed to the appraiser and the details provided must comply with B4-1.1-04, Unacceptable Appraisal Practices. The appraiser must determine if the information could affect either the marketability of the property or the opinion of the market value of the property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Sales Contract Information
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: All financing data and sales concessions for the subject property that will be or have been granted by anyone associated with the transaction must be disclosed to the appraiser, as appropriate. Typically, this information is provided in the sales contract. Therefore, the lender must provide, or ensure that the appraiser is provided with, a copy of the complete, ratified sales contract and all addenda for the property that is to be appraised.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Information Disclosed to the Appraiser
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Financial Information
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The list below includes items that must be disclosed to the appraiser on purchase transactions, if applicable:
elements.label: text

elements.text: settlement charges,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: loan fees or charges,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: discounts to the sales price,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: interest rate buydowns,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: below-market-rate financing,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: terms of any subordinate financing provided by interested parties,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: credits or refunds of borrower expenses,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: absorption of monthly payments,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: assignment of rent payments, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any other information not listed above that impacts property value.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Property Information
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The list below includes items that must be disclosed, if applicable:
elements.label: text

elements.text: condo or PUD fees;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: non-realty items included in the transaction;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any environmental hazard in or on the subject property or in the vicinity of the property that the lender is aware of or learns from the borrower, the real estate agent, or any other party to the transaction (see B4-1.4-08, Environmental Hazards Appraisal Requirements); and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: any other items that affect the safety, soundness, or structural integrity of a property of which the lender may be aware.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Contract Changes After the Appraisal is Completed
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: If the contract is amended after the effective date of the appraisal in a way that does not affect the description of the property, then the lender is not required to provide the amended contract to the appraiser nor obtain a revised appraisal. Some examples of amendments that do not require the lender to provide the amended contract nor obtain revisions to the already-completed appraisal report include:
elements.label: text

elements.text: sale price,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: transaction terms,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: financing concessions,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: seller-paid closing costs,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: names or initials,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: closing date, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: correction of minor clerical errors such as misspellings.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders using UAD 3.6 must follow the requirements in the UAD 3.6 Policy Supplement .

elements.text: B4-1.1-06, Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) and the Uniform Collateral Data Portal (UCDP) (06/04/2025)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on the Uniform Appraisal Dataset and the Uniform Collateral Data Portal, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Collateral Data Portal (UCDP)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The UAD standardizes appraisal data elements for a subset of fields on specific appraisal report forms and includes all data elements required to complete these forms. Fannie Mae requires that the following appraisal report forms are completed utilizing Appendix D of the UAD Specification when reporting the results of an appraisal for a conventional loan:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report ( Form 1004),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (Desktop) (Form 1004 Desktop)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (Hybrid) (Form 1004 Hybrid)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Individual Condominium Unit Appraisal Report ( Form 1073), and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Individual Condominium Unit Appraisal Report (Hybrid) (Form 1073 Hybrid).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Other appraisal report forms may be completed using the standards contained in the UAD Specification to the extent those standards are applicable to that particular form. Lenders may obtain the most recent version of Appendix D of the Uniform Appraisal Dataset Specification and additional information on Fannie Mae's website.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Uniform Collateral Data Portal (UCDP)
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The UCDP is a portal through which lenders are required to electronically submit appraisal reports for conventional loans sold to Fannie Mae. The following appraisal report forms including all exhibits, addenda, and photographs, must be submitted through the UCDP and receive a 'Successful' status from the UCDP prior to sale of the loan to Fannie Mae:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report ( Form 1004),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (Desktop) (Form 1004 Desktop),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (Hybrid) (Form 1004 Hybrid)
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Manufactured Home Appraisal Report ( Form 1004C),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Small Residential Income Property Appraisal Report (Form 1025),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Individual Condominium Unit Appraisal Report ( Form 1073),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Individual Condominium Unit Appraisal Report (Hybrid) ( Form 1073 Hybrid), and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Individual Cooperative Interest Appraisal Report ( Form 2090).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: If there are subsequent revisions to the appraisal report, the final version of the report that was utilized in making the underwriting decision must be submitted through the UCDP and receive a 'Successful' status from the UCDP prior to sale of the loan to Fannie Mae. When submitting an appraisal report through the UCDP, lenders must ensure it is the unaltered report submitted by the identified appraiser.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Additionally, for loans that require an appraisal, lenders must ensure the appraised value as indicated on the appraisal submitted to UCDP matches the appraised value as reported at sale of the loan. An exception is allowed for this requirement when the appraisal used to underwrite the loan is a desk or field review of an existing appraisal because those types of reports cannot be submitted through the UCDP. In those instances, the appraised value reported at sale of the loan will reflect the value as stated in the desk or field review. However, the original appraisal that was the subject of review must have been submitted to UCDP.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Appraisal report forms not listed above cannot be delivered through UCDP with the exception of the Appraisal Update and/or Completion Certificate ( Form 1004D ), which is optional. Lenders must maintain the applicable appraisal report and attachments in the loan file as part of the underwriting documents in accordance with A2-4.1-02, Ownership and Retention of Loan Files and Records. Lenders may obtain detailed information on the UCDP page on Fannie Mae's website .

elements.text: Scope of Work
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Appraisers must use the most recent version of the appraisal report forms and include any other information, either as an attachment or addendum to the appraisal report form, needed to adequately support the opinion of market value. Although the scope of work for the appraisal or the extent of the appraisal process is guided by Fannie Mae's appraisal report forms, the forms do not limit or control the appraisal process. The appraiser's analysis should go beyond any limitations of the forms, with additional comments and exhibits being used if they are needed to adequately describe the subject property, document the analysis and valuation process, or support the appraiser's conclusions. The extent of the appraiser's data collection, analysis, and reporting must be determined by the complexity of the appraisal assignment.

elements.text: Appraiser Certifications and Limiting Conditions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Each Fannie Mae appraisal report form includes an appraiser's certification (and, if applicable, a supervisory appraiser's certification) and a statement of assumptions and limiting conditions. Appraisers may not add limiting conditions.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The appraiser may not make changes or deletions to the existing certifications; however, the appraiser may make additional certifications that can be included on a separate page or form. Acceptable additional certifications might include:
elements.label: text

elements.text: those required by state law;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: those related to the appraiser's continuing education or membership in an appraisal organization; or
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: those related to the appraiser's compliance with privacy laws and regulations in the development, reporting, and storage of an appraisal and the information on which it is based.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Lenders are responsible for reviewing any additional certifications made by appraisers to ensure that they do not conflict with Fannie Mae's policies or standard certifications on Fannie Mae appraisal forms.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The appraiser's certification #23 is an acknowledgment by the appraiser that certain parties to a mortgage finance transaction that are not the lender/client and/or intended user may rely on the appraisal report. This certification clarifies that such other parties include the borrower, another lender at the request of the borrower, the mortgagee or its successors and assigns, mortgage insurers, government-sponsored enterprises, and other secondary market participants.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Fannie Mae will accept the following additional notice or statement when appraisers believe the lender/client is the only intended user:
elements.label: text

elements.text: 'The intended user of this appraisal report is the lender/client. The intended use is to evaluate the property that is the subject of this appraisal for a mortgage finance transaction, subject to the stated scope of work, purpose of the appraisal, reporting requirements of this appraisal report form, and definition of market value. No additional intended users are identified by the appraiser.'

elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Introduction
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: This topic contains information on desktop appraisals, including:
elements.label: text

elements.text: Overview

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For certain loan casefiles, DU offers desktop appraisals. A desktop appraisal reported on the Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (Desktop) (Form 1004 Desktop) is permitted for certain transactions. The minimum scope of work for Form 1004 Desktop does not include a current inspection of the subject property or comparable sales by the appraiser; the appraiser relies on data obtained from alternative methods or sources to identify property characteristics and condition.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Completing the Form 1004 Desktop
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Subject property information may be obtained from one or more data sources. The appraiser can accept data, photos, floor plans, and other information from a party who has a financial interest in the sale or financing of the property if the appraiser verifies such data from a disinterested source. The appraiser must determine if the information is accurate and reliable to produce a credible report, which includes the features, quality, and condition of the subject property.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Virtual inspection methods (including digital photos or videos) or other technological solutions (such as a machine-generated floor plan) can augment the data and imagery used for a desktop appraisal. Information provided by the homeowner, potential borrower, or a third party can be used to develop the description of the interior and exterior of the improvements. Extraordinary assumption(s) or appraisals made "subject to" verification of the subject property's condition, quality, or physical characteristics are not permitted.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender remains responsible for the description of the property, and the accuracy and of all data on the appraisal that pertains to the property. This includes the property's condition and quality ratings. The lender is also responsible for ensuring the property meets the property eligibility requirements in this Selling Guide . Lastly, the lender remains responsible for any life-of-loan representations and warranties that may apply to the property or the appraisal.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Exhibits: Form 1004 Desktop requires the same exhibits as traditional appraisals, plus a floor plan. The Square Footage-Method for Calculating: ANSI Z765-2021 standard is not required for desktop appraisals but encouraged when feasible. See B4-1.2-01, Appraisal Report Forms and Exhibits for additional information.
elements.label: text

elements.text: DU Messaging: For loan casefiles that are eligible for a desktop appraisal option, DU will issue a message informing the lender they can choose to obtain an appraisal reported on Form 1004 Desktop. DU will also issue messages for other appraisal options. The lender may select from any of the options presented, which may include a desktop appraisal, hybrid appraisal, or traditional appraisal.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligible Transactions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: To be eligible for a desktop appraisal, transactions must meet the following criteria:
elements.label: text

elements.text: one-unit property (including those with an ADU and units in a PUD),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: principal residence,
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: purchase transaction (including new construction),
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: LTV ratio less than or equal to 90%, and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU loan casefile that receives an Approve/Eligible recommendation.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Ineligible Transactions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following transactions are not eligible for a desktop appraisal:
elements.label: text

elements.text: two- to four-unit properties;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: condo and co-op units;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: manufactured homes;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: construction-to-permanent loans (single-close and two-close);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: second homes and investment properties;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: all refinances;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: HomeReady, HomeStyle Renovation, and HomeStyle Refresh loans;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Community Seconds with a subsidized sales price;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: community land trusts, or other properties with resale price restrictions, which include loan casefiles using the Affordable LTV feature;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU loan casefiles that receive an Ineligible recommendation; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: manually underwritten loans.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Representations and Warranties
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a desktop appraisal is reported on Form 1004 Desktop and submitted through the UCDP, the appraisal will be scored by Collateral Underwriter (CU). All eligible loans with a desktop appraisal that receive a CU risk score of 2.5 or less are eligible for enforcement relief of certain representations and warranties related to the appraisal value of the subject property (see  A2-2-06, Representations and Warranties on Property Value  for complete requirements and additional information).

elements.label: list_item

elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Overview
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: For certain loan casefiles, DU offers hybrid appraisals - an appraisal option requiring a property data collection. A hybrid appraisal reported on the Uniform Residential Appraisal Report (Hybrid) (Form 1004 Hybrid) or Individual Condominium Unit Appraisal Report (Hybrid) (Form 1073 Hybrid) must conform to B4-1.1-04, Unacceptable Appraisal Practices. The minimum scope of work for hybrid appraisals includes consideration by the appraiser of interior and exterior property data collection by a trained and vetted third party (such as a real estate agent, insurance inspector, appraiser, etc.). The appraiser relies on the data collected (and other sources if needed) to identify property characteristics including condition. The property data collection must comply with the Uniform Property Dataset and be delivered to the Fannie Mae Property Data API.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Completing the Hybrid Appraisal
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Property data collection and the appraisal report are separate assignments and may be performed by different people. If the appraiser does not perform the data collection, the lender must share the property data collection with the appraiser at the time of engagement. The appraiser will use the data along with other third-party sources to develop the appraisal. The effective date of the hybrid appraisal is the date that the appraiser arrives at their opinion of value.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Note: If the appraiser is engaged as a property data collector and at a later date engaged as an appraiser, this is still considered a hybrid appraisal.
elements.label: text

elements.text: The lender remains responsible for verifying the accuracy of the property description and the completeness of the data including the condition and quality ratings as determined by the appraiser. The lender is also responsible for ensuring the property meets the property eligibility requirements in this Selling Guide . Lastly, the lender remains responsible for any life-of-loan representations and warranties that may apply to the property or the appraisal.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Exhibits: Form 1004 Hybrid and Form 1073 Hybrid require the same exhibits as traditional appraisals with the inclusion of a floor plan conforming to the ANSI standard. See B4-1.2-01, Appraisal Report Forms and Exhibits for additional information on exhibits and B4-1.3-05, Improvements Section of the Appraisal Report for additional information on the ANSI standard.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: DU Messaging: For loan casefiles that are eligible for a hybrid appraisal option, DU will issue a message informing the lender they can obtain an appraisal reported on Form 1004 Hybrid or Form 1073 Hybrid. DU will also issue messages for other appraisal options. The lender may select from any of the options presented, which may include a hybrid appraisal, desktop appraisal, or traditional appraisal.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Eligible Transactions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following are eligible for a hybrid appraisal:
elements.label: text

elements.text: existing one-unit properties, including a condo or unit in a PUD;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: principal residence, second home, or investment properties;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: under construction properties;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: existing properties with incomplete construction or renovation project;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: community land trusts, or other properties with resale price restrictions, which include loan casefiles using the Affordable LTV feature; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Texas 50(a)(6) loans.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: The transaction must be a purchase, limited cash-out refinance, or cash-out refinance.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Published June 3, 2026
elements.label: page_footer

elements.text: Ineligible Transactions
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: The following transactions are not eligible for a hybrid appraisal:
elements.label: text

elements.text: two- to four-unit properties;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: co-op units;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: manufactured homes;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: proposed construction;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: construction-to-permanent loans (single-close and two-close);
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: HomeStyle Renovation and HomeStyle Refresh loans;
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: DU loan casefiles that receive an Ineligible recommendation; and
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: manually underwritten loans.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Exercising Hybrid Appraisals
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When exercising a hybrid appraisal, the lender must complete all of the following:
elements.label: text

elements.text: obtain a property data collection and successfully submit to the Fannie Mae Property Data API.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: provide the property data collection to an appraiser for the completion of a hybrid appraisal based on property type using either Form 1004 Hybrid or Form 1073 Hybrid.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: submit the hybrid appraisal to the Uniform Collateral Data Portal (UCDP).
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: perform an appraisal review in accordance with this Guide.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: deliver the loan with the hybrid appraisal to Fannie Mae.
elements.label: list_item

elements.text: Note: A lender may obtain a hybrid appraisal if a loan loses value acceptance + property data eligibility after submitting the property data collection to the Property Data API. See B4-1.4-11, Value Acceptance + Property Data B4-1.4-11, Value Acceptance + Property Datafor specific requirements.
elements.label: text

elements.text: Representations and Warranties
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: When a hybrid appraisal is reported on Form 1004 Hybrid or Form 1073 Hybrid and submitted through the UCDP, the appraisal will be scored by Collateral Underwriter (CU). All eligible loans with a hybrid appraisal receiving a CU risk score of 2.5 or less are eligible for enforcement relief of certain representations and warranties related to the appraisal value of the subject property (see A2-2-06, Representations and Warranties on Property Value for complete requirements and additional information).
elements.label: text

elements.text: Uniform Appraisal Dataset (UAD) 3.6 Policy
elements.label: section_header

elements.text: Lenders using UAD 3.6 must follow the requirements in the UAD 3.6 Policy Supplement .
